title: command_injection
description: An attacker exploits an unsanitised diagnostic endpoint to inject OS commands, gaining remote code execution on the web server.

timeline.title: Suspicious Diagnostic Requests
timeline.description: WAF logs show unusual requests to /api/diagnostic/ping with shell metacharacters in the host parameter. Responses contain OS command output.

timeline.title: Remote Code Execution Confirmed
timeline.description: Analysis confirms the attacker is executing arbitrary OS commands as the web server service account. A reverse shell payload is attempted.

timeline.title: Reverse Shell Established
timeline.description: The attacker successfully establishes an outbound reverse shell from the web server to an external C2 server at 198.51.100.10.

timeline.title: Server Isolated
timeline.description: The web server is isolated from the network. Reverse shell connection terminated. Forensic imaging initiated.

timeline.title: Endpoint Patched
timeline.description: Vulnerable diagnostic endpoint removed. Input validation and allowlist-based parameter checking deployed on all API endpoints.

timeline.title: Application Hardened
timeline.description: Web server process privilege reduced. Egress firewall rules block outbound reverse shells. Full code review completed.

node-1.title: Command Injection via Diagnostic API
node-1.situation: WAF logs show requests to /api/diagnostic/ping?host=127.0.0.1;id containing shell metacharacters. The response includes the output of the id command, confirming OS command injection. The attacker has RCE as the web server service account.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Take the vulnerable endpoint offline immediately by disabling the /api/diagnostic/ route, block outbound connections from the web server at the firewall, and alert the IR team.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a WAF rule to block the specific payload pattern used and continue monitoring while the endpoint remains active.

options.label: C
options.text: Reboot the web server to terminate any active sessions and assess damage afterwards.

node-2.title: Reverse Shell Activity Detected
node-2.situation: Network logs show an outbound connection from the web server to 198.51.100.10:4444 (known C2 server). A reverse shell was established before your firewall block took effect. The attacker may have accessed sensitive files or installed persistence.
node-2.question: How do you respond to the active compromise?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolate the web server from the network entirely, preserve a forensic image before any remediation, and analyse running processes and filesystem for backdoors or exfiltrated data.

options.label: B
options.text: Kill the reverse shell process and restore normal web server operations without forensic imaging.

node-3.title: Forensic Analysis and Scope Assessment
node-3.situation: Forensic analysis reveals the attacker accessed the web server's /etc/passwd, application config files (including database credentials), and attempted to install a cron-based persistence mechanism.
node-3.question: How do you address the exposed credentials and persistence?

options.label: A
options.text: Rotate all credentials found in the application config files, audit the database for unauthorised access, remove persistence mechanisms, and rebuild the web server from a clean image.

options.label: B
options.text: Change only the database password and restore the web server from the last backup without further investigation.

node-4.title: Harden Against Command Injection
node-4.situation: The vulnerability existed because the diagnostic endpoint passed user input directly to a shell function without sanitisation. You must prevent command injection across the entire application.
node-4.question: What security controls do you implement?

options.label: A
options.text: Remove all shell execution functions from application code, run the web server as a least-privilege account, implement egress firewall controls, and integrate SAST to detect injection patterns in CI/CD.

options.label: B
options.text: Add input length limits to API parameters as the sole remediation to prevent command injection.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The command injection exploit was contained. The compromised web server was forensically analysed, credentials rotated, and the server rebuilt. Least-privilege process accounts, egress firewall controls, and SAST tooling now prevent future command injection attacks.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
